Two pointers

  • Ref
    • fucking-algorithm : 2 pointers

0) Concept

0-1) Types

  • Pointer types

    • Fast - Slow pointers

      • fast, slow pointers from same start point

    • Left- Right pointers

      • left, right pointers from idx = 0, idx = len(n) - 1 respectively
      • Usually set
        • left pointer = 0
        • right pointer = len(nums)
      • binary search
      • array reverse
      • 2 sum
      • sliding window

  • Expand from center (and Deal with odd, even cases)

    • LC 680
    • LC 647
    • LC 005

  • Merge Sorted Array

    • LC 88

  • Minimum Swaps to Group All 1’s Together

    • LC 1151 (check sliding_window.md)

  • Boats to Save People

    • LC 881

  • move right pointer first, then move left point per condition

    • LC 567
    • LC 209 (see sliding window cheatsheet)

  • Subsequence Matching with character type constraints

    • One pointer always moves, one conditionally moves
    • Extra validation on non-matching characters
    • LC 392 (Is Subsequence)
    • LC 1023 (Camelcase Matching - with uppercase/lowercase constraint)

  • Algorithm

    • binary search
    • sliding window
    • for loop + “expand left, right from center”

  • Data structure

    • Array
    • Linked list

0-2) Pattern

0-2-0) Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array

Core Idea

Slow-Fast (Write-Read) Pattern:

  • slow = the “write” pointer — tracks the last confirmed unique position
  • fast = the “read” pointer — scans the array looking for new unique values
  • When nums[fast] != nums[slow]: a new unique value is found
    1. Advance slow first (open the next write slot)
    2. Write (or swap) nums[fast] into nums[slow]
  • Return slow + 1 as the count of unique elements

Key invariant: nums[0..slow] always contains unique elements in sorted order.

Two variants:

  • Overwrite (nums[slow] = nums[fast]): cleaner, preferred — array is already sorted so we just need to copy unique values forward
  • Swap (swap(nums[slow], nums[fast])): also correct but unnecessary for sorted arrays; used when original values need to be preserved elsewhere
1Pointer movement rules:
2  - fast: moves EVERY iteration (scans all elements)
3  - slow: moves ONLY when a new unique value is found (after nums[fast] != nums[slow])
4  - Both start at 0 (or slow=0, fast=1 in while-loop variants)

1// java
2// LC 26 (LC 83)
3// https://labuladong.online/algo/essential-technique/array-two-pointers-summary/#%E5%8E%9F%E5%9C%B0%E4%BF%AE%E6%94%B9
4/**
5 *  //--------------------------------
6 *  Example 1
7 *  //--------------------------------
8 *
9 *  nums = [1,1,2]
10 *
11 *  [1,1,2]
12 *   s f
13 *
14 *  [1,2, 1]     if nums[f] != nums[s], move s, then swap f, s
15 *   s s  f
16 *
17 *
18 *   //--------------------------------
19 *   Example 2
20 *   //--------------------------------
21 *
22 *   nums = [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
23 *
24 *   [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
25 *    s f
26 *
27 *   [0,1,0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]   if nums[f] != nums[s], move s, then swap f, s
28 *    s s f
29 *
30 *   [0,1,0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
31 *      s   f
32 *
33 *   [0,1,0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
34 *      s     f
35 *
36 *   [0,1,2,1,1,0,2,3,3,4]   if nums[f] != nums[s], move s, then swap f, s
37 *      s s     f
38 *
39 *   [0,1,2,1,1,0,2,3,3,4]
40 *        s       f
41 *
42 *   [0,1,2,3,1,0,2,1,3,4]  if nums[f] != nums[s], move s, then swap f, s
43 *        s s       f
44 *
45 *   [0,1,2,3,1,0,2,1,3,4]
46 *          s         f
47 *
48 *   [0,1,2,3,4,0,2,1,3,1]   if nums[f] != nums[s], move s, then swap f, s
49 *          s s         f
50 *
51 */
52// Variant A: OVERWRITE (cleaner, preferred for sorted arrays)
53class Solution {
54    public int removeDuplicates(int[] nums) {
55        if (nums.length == 0) return 0;
56
57        int slow = 0;
58        for (int fast = 1; fast < nums.length; fast++) {
59            if (nums[fast] != nums[slow]) {
60                slow++;                  // open next write slot
61                nums[slow] = nums[fast]; // overwrite with new unique value
62            }
63            // if equal: fast keeps moving, slow stays
64        }
65        return slow + 1;
66    }
67}
68
69// Variant B: SWAP (preserves all values, same time/space)
70class Solution {
71    public int removeDuplicates(int[] nums) {
72        if (nums.length == 0) return 0;
73
74        int slow = 0, fast = 0;
75        while (fast < nums.length) {
76            if (nums[fast] != nums[slow]) {
77                slow++;
78                // swap: move the new unique value to slow position
79                int tmp = nums[slow];
80                nums[slow] = nums[fast];
81                nums[fast] = tmp;
82            }
83            fast++;
84        }
85        return slow + 1;
86    }
87}

Pattern Summary

Step Action Why
nums[fast] == nums[slow] Only advance fast Duplicate — skip it
nums[fast] != nums[slow] slow++, then write/swap New unique found — claim next slot
Return slow + 1 slow is index, length = index + 1

Why overwrite instead of swap?

  • Array is sorted → we only move values left, never right
  • No need to preserve overwritten values (they are duplicates already seen)
  • nums[slow] = nums[fast] is O(1) and simpler

Similar Problems

Problem LC# Difference Key Trick
Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array 26 Allow each value once nums[slow] = nums[fast] when different
Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II 80 Allow each value at most twice Compare nums[fast] with nums[slow-1] (two back)
Remove Element 27 Remove all occurrences of val Write when nums[fast] != val
Move Zeroes 283 Move zeros to end, preserve order Swap when nums[fast] != 0
Remove Duplicates from Sorted List 83 Linked list version of LC 26 node.next = node.next.next on duplicate
Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II 82 Delete ALL nodes with duplicate values Extra sentinel node + skip entire duplicate group

0-2-0b) Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II (LC 80)

Core Idea

“Compare with two positions back” trick:

  • Allow each element at most twice → keep an element only if it differs from nums[slow - 2]
  • Since the array is sorted, if nums[fast] == nums[slow - 2], writing it would create a 3rd consecutive duplicate → skip
  • Both slow and fast start at index 2 (first two elements always allowed)
1Key condition: nums[fast] != nums[slow - 2]
2  → write nums[fast] to nums[slow], slow++
3  
4Pointer initialization:
5  slow = 2  (write pointer, first 2 slots are always valid)
6  fast = 2  (read pointer, scans from index 2 onward)

Why slow - 2 and not slow - 1?

  • slow - 1 would only prevent 3rd+ duplicates if the LAST two wrote the same value
  • slow - 2 directly checks if the slot two positions back already holds the same value — guaranteeing at most 2 copies
1// java
2// LC 80 - Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II
3// time: O(N), space: O(1)
4/**
5 *  //--------------------------------
6 *  Example 1
7 *  //--------------------------------
8 *
9 *  nums = [1,1,1,2,2,3]
10 *
11 *  Initial: slow=2, fast=2
12 *  [1,1,1,2,2,3]
13 *       s
14 *       f
15 *
16 *  fast=2: nums[2]=1, nums[slow-2]=nums[0]=1  → EQUAL, skip  (would be 3rd '1')
17 *  fast=3: nums[3]=2, nums[slow-2]=nums[0]=1  → DIFFERENT, write nums[slow]=2, slow=3
18 *  [1,1,2,2,2,3]
19 *         s
20 *           f
21 *
22 *  fast=4: nums[4]=2, nums[slow-2]=nums[1]=1  → DIFFERENT, write, slow=4
23 *  [1,1,2,2,2,3]
24 *           s
25 *             f
26 *
27 *  fast=5: nums[5]=3, nums[slow-2]=nums[2]=2  → DIFFERENT, write, slow=5
28 *  [1,1,2,2,3,3]
29 *             s
30 *
31 *  return slow = 5  → nums[0..4] = [1,1,2,2,3]
32 *
33 *  //--------------------------------
34 *  Example 2
35 *  //--------------------------------
36 *
37 *  nums = [0,0,1,1,1,1,2,3,3]
38 *
39 *  Initial: slow=2, fast=2
40 *  fast=2: nums[2]=1, nums[0]=0 → DIFFERENT, write, slow=3
41 *  fast=3: nums[3]=1, nums[1]=0 → DIFFERENT, write, slow=4
42 *  fast=4: nums[4]=1, nums[2]=1 → EQUAL, skip  (3rd '1')
43 *  fast=5: nums[5]=1, nums[2]=1 → EQUAL, skip  (4th '1')
44 *  fast=6: nums[6]=2, nums[2]=1 → DIFFERENT, write, slow=5
45 *  fast=7: nums[7]=3, nums[3]=1 → DIFFERENT, write, slow=6
46 *  fast=8: nums[8]=3, nums[4]=1 → DIFFERENT, write, slow=7
47 *
48 *  return slow = 7  → nums[0..6] = [0,0,1,1,2,3,3]
49 */
50public int removeDuplicates(int[] nums) {
51    if (nums.length <= 2) return nums.length;
52
53    int slow = 2; // write pointer; first 2 elements always valid
54    for (int fast = 2; fast < nums.length; fast++) {
55        // Only write if current element != element two slots back
56        if (nums[fast] != nums[slow - 2]) {
57            nums[slow] = nums[fast];
58            slow++;
59        }
60        // else: would create 3rd duplicate → skip
61    }
62    return slow;
63}

Generalized Pattern: Allow at most K duplicates

1// Generic template: allow each element at most K times
2// LC 26 is K=1, LC 80 is K=2
3public int removeDuplicatesAtMostK(int[] nums, int k) {
4    int slow = k;
5    for (int fast = k; fast < nums.length; fast++) {
6        if (nums[fast] != nums[slow - k]) {
7            nums[slow] = nums[fast];
8            slow++;
9        }
10    }
11    return slow;
12}
13// LC 26: call with k=1  →  compare nums[fast] != nums[slow - 1]
14// LC 80: call with k=2  →  compare nums[fast] != nums[slow - 2]

LC 26 vs LC 80 Comparison

Aspect LC 26 (at most 1) LC 80 (at most 2)
Condition nums[fast] != nums[slow - 1] nums[fast] != nums[slow - 2]
Init slow = 1, fast = 1 slow = 2, fast = 2
Returns slow slow
Generalized k = 1 k = 2

Similar Problems

Problem LC# Key Difference
Remove Duplicates I 26 At most 1 copy — compare nums[slow-1]
Remove Duplicates II 80 At most 2 copies — compare nums[slow-2]
Remove Element 27 Remove all of a specific value
Move Zeroes 283 Keep zeros, move to end

0-2-1) Remove Element

1// java
2// LC 27
3// https://labuladong.online/algo/essential-technique/array-two-pointers-summary/#%E5%8E%9F%E5%9C%B0%E4%BF%AE%E6%94%B9
4/**
5 *  //--------------------
6 *  Example 1
7 *  //--------------------
8 *
9 *  nums = [3,2,2,3], val = 3
10 *
11 *  [3,2,2,3]
12 *   s
13 *   f
14 *
15 *  [2,3,2,3]    if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
16 *   s s
17 *     f
18 *
19 *  [2,2,3,3]   if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
20 *     s s
21 *       f
22 *
23 * [2,2,3,3]
24 *      s
25 *        f
26 *
27 *
28 *  //--------------------
29 *  Example 2
30 *  //--------------------
31 *
32 *  nums = [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2], val = 2
33 *
34 *
35 *  [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]   if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
36 *   s s
37 *   f
38 *
39 *  [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]     if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
40 *     s s
41 *     f
42 *
43 *  [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]
44 *       s
45 *       f
46 *
47 * [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]
48 *      s
49 *        f
50 *
51 * [0,1,3,2,2,0,4,2]   if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
52 *      s s
53 *          f
54 *
55 * [0,1,3,0,2,2,4,2]   if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
56 *        s s
57 *            f
58 *
59 * [0,1,3,0,4,2,2,2]    if nums[f] != val, swap, move s
60 *          s s
61 *              f
62 *
63 *  [0,1,3,0,4,2,2,2]
64 *             s
65 *                 f
66 */
67class Solution {
68    public int removeElement(int[] nums, int val) {
69        int fast = 0, slow = 0;
70        while (fast < nums.length) {
71            if (nums[fast] != val) {
72                nums[slow] = nums[fast];
73                slow++;
74            }
75            fast++;
76        }
77        return slow;
78    }
79}

0-2-1b) Remove Element (Bidirectional Two Pointers)

Pattern: Left-Right pointers, shrink from both ends

Key difference from the fast-slow (0-2-1) approach:

  • Fast-slow overwrites sequentially → preserves relative order
  • Bidirectional replaces nums[l] with nums[r] → does NOT preserve order, but may do fewer writes (good when val is rare)
1// java
2// LC 27 Remove Element - Bidirectional variant
3/**
4 * Key Idea:
5 *   - l starts at 0, r starts at nums.length - 1
6 *   - If nums[l] == val, OVERWRITE it with nums[r] and shrink r
7 *     (do NOT advance l yet — the new nums[l] might also be val)
8 *   - If nums[l] != val, it is a "good" element → advance l
9 *   - When l > r, l equals the count of valid elements
10 *
11 * //--------------------
12 * Example 1
13 * //--------------------
14 * nums = [3,2,2,3], val = 3
15 *
16 * [3,2,2,3]   nums[l]=3==val, nums[l]=nums[r]=3, r--
17 *  l     r
18 *
19 * [3,2,2,3]   nums[l]=3==val, nums[l]=nums[r]=2, r--
20 *  l   r
21 *
22 * [2,2,2,3]   nums[l]=2!=val, l++
23 *  l r
24 *
25 * [2,2,2,3]   nums[l]=2!=val, l++
26 *    lr
27 *
28 * l(2) > r(1), return l = 2
29 *
30 * //--------------------
31 * Example 2
32 * //--------------------
33 * nums = [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2], val = 2
34 *
35 * [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=0!=val, l++
36 *  l               r
37 *
38 * [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=1!=val, l++
39 *    l             r
40 *
41 * [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=2==val, nums[l]=nums[r]=2, r--
42 *      l           r
43 *
44 * [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=2==val, nums[l]=nums[r]=4, r--
45 *      l         r
46 *
47 * [0,1,4,2,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=4!=val, l++
48 *      l       r
49 *
50 * [0,1,4,2,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=2==val, nums[l]=nums[r]=0, r--
51 *        l     r
52 *
53 * [0,1,4,0,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=0!=val, l++
54 *        l   r
55 *
56 * [0,1,4,0,3,0,4,2]   nums[l]=3!=val, l++
57 *          l r
58 *
59 * l(5) > r(4), return l = 5
60 *
61 * Time: O(N), Space: O(1)
62 */
63public int removeElement(int[] nums, int val) {
64    int l = 0;
65    int r = nums.length - 1;
66
67    while (l <= r) {
68        if (nums[l] == val) {
69            // Overwrite with rightmost element, shrink right boundary
70            // NOTE: do NOT advance l — new nums[l] might also be val
71            nums[l] = nums[r];
72            r--;
73        } else {
74            // Good element confirmed, advance left
75            l++;
76        }
77    }
78
79    // l is exactly the count of non-val elements
80    return l;
81}

Comparison: Fast-Slow vs Bidirectional

Aspect Fast-Slow (0-2-1) Bidirectional (this)
Order Preserves relative order Does NOT preserve order
Writes One write per valid element Fewer writes when val is rare
Loop style for loop (fast advances always) while (l <= r)
When to use Order matters Order doesn’t matter, minimal writes

Similar Problems:

  • LC 27 Remove Element (this pattern)
  • LC 905 Sort Array By Parity — move evens left, odds right (same bidirectional shrink idea)
  • LC 75 Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag) — three-way bidirectional partition
  • LC 283 Move Zeroes — order matters, use fast-slow instead
  • LC 26 Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array — order matters, use fast-slow instead
  • LC 80 Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II — order matters, use fast-slow instead

0-2-2) Move Zeros to End

1// java
2// LC 283 Move Zeroes
3// https://leetcode.com/problems/move-zeroes/
4/**
5 * Pattern: Move all zeros (or specific elements) to the end while maintaining
6 * the relative order of non-zero elements. Must be done in-place.
7 *
8 * Key Idea:
9 *   - Both pointers (l and r) start from index 0
10 *   - l tracks the position where the next non-zero should be placed
11 *   - r scans through the array
12 *   - When r finds a non-zero, swap with l and move l forward
13 *   - This moves all zeros to the end naturally
14 *
15 * Difference from "Remove Element" pattern:
16 *   - Remove Element: overwrites without caring about moved elements
17 *   - Move Zeros: uses SWAP to preserve all elements in array
18 *
19 *  //--------------------
20 *  Example 1
21 *  //--------------------
22 *
23 *  nums = [0,1,0,3,12]
24 *
25 *  [0,1,0,3,12]
26 *   l
27 *   r
28 *
29 *  [0,1,0,3,12]    nums[r]=0, no swap, move r
30 *   l
31 *     r
32 *
33 *  [1,0,0,3,12]    nums[r]!=0, swap(l,r), move l and r
34 *   l l
35 *     r
36 *
37 *  [1,0,0,3,12]    nums[r]=0, no swap, move r
38 *     l
39 *       r
40 *
41 *  [1,3,0,0,12]    nums[r]!=0, swap(l,r), move l and r
42 *     l l
43 *         r
44 *
45 *  [1,3,12,0,0]    nums[r]!=0, swap(l,r), move l and r
46 *        l  l
47 *            r
48 *
49 *  //--------------------
50 *  Example 2
51 *  //--------------------
52 *
53 *  nums = [0]
54 *  [0]
55 *   l
56 *   r
57 *  -> only one element, no change
58 *
59 *  //--------------------
60 *  Example 3
61 *  //--------------------
62 *
63 *  nums = [1,0,2,0,3]
64 *
65 *  [1,0,2,0,3]    nums[r]!=0, swap(l,r), move l and r
66 *   l l
67 *   r
68 *
69 *  [1,0,2,0,3]    nums[r]=0, no swap, move r
70 *     l
71 *     r
72 *
73 *  [1,2,0,0,3]    nums[r]!=0, swap(l,r), move l and r
74 *     l l
75 *       r
76 *
77 *  [1,2,0,0,3]    nums[r]=0, no swap, move r
78 *       l
79 *         r
80 *
81 *  [1,2,3,0,0]    nums[r]!=0, swap(l,r), move l and r
82 *       l l
83 *           r
84 *
85 * Time: O(N), Space: O(1)
86 */
87class Solution {
88    public void moveZeroes(int[] nums) {
89        if (nums == null || nums.length <= 1)
90            return;
91
92        // 'l' is the position where the next non-zero number should be placed
93        int l = 0;
94
95        /** NOTE !!!
96         *
97         *  BOTH l, r start from idx = 0
98         */
99        // Iterate through the array with 'r'
100        for (int r = 0; r < nums.length; r++) {
101            // If we find a non-zero element
102            if (nums[r] != 0) {
103                // Swap it with the element at position 'l'
104                int tmp = nums[r];
105                nums[r] = nums[l];
106                nums[l] = tmp;
107
108                /** NOTE !!!
109                 *
110                 *  Move 'l' forward if `we swap`
111                 */
112                // Move 'l' forward
113                l++;
114            }
115        }
116    }
117}

Similar Problems:

  • LC 283 Move Zeroes (this pattern)
  • LC 27 Remove Element (overwrite version)
  • LC 905 Sort Array By Parity (move even numbers to front)
  • LC 922 Sort Array By Parity II (even/odd positioning)
  • LC 2460 Apply Operations to an Array
  • LC 1089 Duplicate Zeros (expanding array version)

0-2-3) for loop + “expand left, right from center”

1# LC 005  Longest Palindromic Substring
2# LC 647 Palindromic Substrings
3# python
4# pseudo code
5# ...
6for i in range(lens(s)):
7
8    # NOTE !!!
9    # NO NEED to have logic like `if i % 2 == 1`..
10    # we can just consider `odd, even len` cases directly
11
12    #--------------------------------------
13    # if odd
14    # NOTE !!! if `odd`, left = right = i
15    #--------------------------------------
16    left = right = i
17    while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]:
18        if right+1-left > len(res):
19            res = s[left:right+1]
20        left -= 1
21        right += 1
22    
23    #--------------------------------------
24    # if even
25    # NOTE !!! if `odd`, left = i - 1, right = i
26    #--------------------------------------
27    left = i - 1
28    right = i
29    while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]:
30        if right+1-left > len(res):
31            res = s[left:right+1]
32        left -= 1
33        right += 1
34# ...

0-2-4) Move right pointer, then move left point

1// java
2// LC 567
3
4int l = 0;
5for (int r = 0; r < s2.length(); r++){
6    // ...
7
8    if(some_condition){
9
10        // ...
11        // update map and move left pointer
12        l += 1;
13    }
14}
15
16
17// ...

0-2-5) Subsequence Matching (One Always Moves, One Conditionally Moves)

1// java
2// LC 392 Is Subsequence
3// https://leetcode.com/problems/is-subsequence/
4
5/**
6 * Pattern: Check if string s is a subsequence of string t
7 *
8 * Key Idea:
9 *   - Use two pointers: i for s (target subsequence), j for t (main string)
10 *   - ALWAYS move j (scan through entire t)
11 *   - ONLY move i when characters match
12 *   - If i reaches end of s, we found all characters in order
13 *
14 * Example:
15 *   s = "abc", t = "ahbgdc"
16 *
17 *   [a h b g d c]    i=0, j=0, s[i]=a, t[j]=a, match! i++, j++
18 *    i j
19 *
20 *   [a h b g d c]    i=1, j=1, s[i]=b, t[j]=h, no match, j++
21 *      i j
22 *
23 *   [a h b g d c]    i=1, j=2, s[i]=b, t[j]=b, match! i++, j++
24 *        i j
25 *
26 *   [a h b g d c]    i=2, j=3, s[i]=c, t[j]=g, no match, j++
27 *          i j
28 *
29 *   [a h b g d c]    i=2, j=4, s[i]=c, t[j]=d, no match, j++
30 *            i j
31 *
32 *   [a h b g d c]    i=2, j=5, s[i]=c, t[j]=c, match! i++, j++
33 *              i j
34 *
35 *   i == s.length() -> return true
36 */
37public boolean isSubsequence(String s, String t) {
38    if (s.isEmpty())
39        return true;
40    if (t.isEmpty())
41        return false;
42
43    int i = 0; // Pointer for s (target subsequence)
44    int j = 0; // Pointer for t (main string)
45
46    /** NOTE !!!
47     *
48     *  the while loop condition:
49     *
50     *     i < s.length()
51     *     &&
52     *     j < t.length()
53     */
54    while (i < s.length() && j < t.length()) {
55        // If characters match, move the pointer for s
56        if (s.charAt(i) == t.charAt(j)) {
57            i++;
58        }
59        // Always move the pointer for t
60        j++;
61    }
62
63    // If i reached the end of s, all characters were found in order
64    return i == s.length();
65}

Classic Problems:

  • LC 392 Is Subsequence
  • LC 524 Longest Word in Dictionary through Deleting
  • LC 792 Number of Matching Subsequences

0-2-6) Pattern Matching with Character Type Constraints (CamelCase Matching)

1// java
2// LC 1023 Camelcase Matching
3// https://leetcode.com/problems/camelcase-matching/
4
5/**
6 * Pattern: Subsequence matching with character type validation
7 *
8 * Key Idea:
9 *   - Similar to subsequence matching, but with EXTRA CONSTRAINT
10 *   - Use two pointers: i for query, j for pattern
11 *   - ALWAYS move i (scan through entire query)
12 *   - ONLY move j when characters match
13 *   - CRITICAL: Any non-matching character in query MUST be lowercase
14 *     (uppercase non-match = invalid)
15 *
16 * Core Logic:
17 *   1. All pattern characters must appear in query in same order (subsequence)
18 *   2. Any extra characters in query MUST be lowercase
19 *   3. If we encounter an extra uppercase letter → immediate failure
20 *
21 * Example 1:
22 *   query = "FooBar", pattern = "FB"
23 *
24 *   [F o o B a r]    i=0, j=0, query[i]=F, pattern[j]=F, match! j++
25 *    i j
26 *
27 *   [F o o B a r]    i=1, j=1, query[i]=o, pattern[j]=B, no match
28 *      i j           but 'o' is lowercase → OK, i++
29 *
30 *   [F o o B a r]    i=2, j=1, query[i]=o, pattern[j]=B, no match
31 *        i j         but 'o' is lowercase → OK, i++
32 *
33 *   [F o o B a r]    i=3, j=1, query[i]=B, pattern[j]=B, match! j++
34 *          i j
35 *
36 *   [F o o B a r]    i=4, j=2, query[i]=a, pattern[j]=none, no match
37 *            i       but 'a' is lowercase → OK, i++
38 *
39 *   [F o o B a r]    i=5, j=2, query[i]=r, pattern[j]=none, no match
40 *              i     but 'r' is lowercase → OK, i++
41 *
42 *   j == pattern.length() → return true
43 *
44 * Example 2:
45 *   query = "FooBarTest", pattern = "FB"
46 *
47 *   ... (matches F, o, o, B, a, r) ...
48 *
49 *   [F o o B a r T e s t]    i=6, j=2, query[i]=T
50 *                  i         'T' is UPPERCASE but not in pattern
51 *                            → return false immediately!
52 *
53 * Pointer Behavior:
54 *   - i (Explorer): Moves EVERY step, scans all characters
55 *   - j (Goal Tracker): ONLY moves when finding matching character
56 *   - Safety Check: Non-matching uppercase → instant failure
57 *
58 * Time: O(M) where M = query length
59 * Space: O(1)
60 */
61public List<Boolean> camelMatch(String[] queries, String pattern) {
62    List<Boolean> result = new ArrayList<>();
63
64    for (String query : queries) {
65        result.add(isMatch(query, pattern));
66    }
67
68    return result;
69}
70
71private boolean isMatch(String query, String pattern) {
72    /** NOTE !!!
73     *
74     *  Two pointers:
75     *    i: query pointer (always moves)
76     *    j: pattern pointer (conditionally moves)
77     */
78    int i = 0; // Pointer for query
79    int j = 0; // Pointer for pattern
80
81    while (i < query.length()) {
82        char qChar = query.charAt(i);
83
84        /** NOTE !!!
85         *
86         *  Three cases:
87         *
88         *  Case 1: Characters match
89         *    → Move both pointers
90         *
91         *  Case 2: Characters don't match AND query char is lowercase
92         *    → OK! This is allowed insertion, move i only
93         *
94         *  Case 3: Characters don't match AND query char is UPPERCASE
95         *    → FAIL! Extra uppercase not allowed
96         */
97
98        // Case 1: If characters match, move the pattern pointer
99        if (j < pattern.length() && qChar == pattern.charAt(j)) {
100            j++;
101        }
102        // Case 3: If characters don't match, the extra character MUST be lowercase
103        else if (Character.isUpperCase(qChar)) {
104            return false;
105        }
106        // Case 2: Lowercase character that doesn't match → skip it
107
108        // Always move the query pointer
109        i++;
110    }
111
112    // Match is only valid if we successfully navigated through the entire pattern
113    return j == pattern.length();
114}

1# python
2# LC 1023 Camelcase Matching
3
4def camelMatch(queries, pattern):
5    """
6    Pattern: Subsequence with character type constraints
7
8    Core Trick:
9      - query pointer ALWAYS moves (explorer)
10      - pattern pointer ONLY moves on match (goal tracker)
11      - Extra validation: non-matching chars MUST be lowercase
12
13    Example:
14      query = "FooBar", pattern = "FB"
15
16      'F' == 'F' → match, j++
17      'o' != 'B' → but lowercase, OK
18      'o' != 'B' → but lowercase, OK
19      'B' == 'B' → match, j++
20      'a' (no pattern) → but lowercase, OK
21      'r' (no pattern) → but lowercase, OK
22
23      j reached end → True
24    """
25    result = []
26
27    for query in queries:
28        i, j = 0, 0
29        is_valid = True
30
31        while i < len(query):
32            # Case 1: Match found
33            if j < len(pattern) and query[i] == pattern[j]:
34                j += 1
35            # Case 2: Uppercase non-match → fail
36            elif query[i].isupper():
37                is_valid = False
38                break
39            # Case 3: Lowercase non-match → skip
40
41            i += 1
42
43        # Valid only if all pattern chars matched
44        result.append(is_valid and j == len(pattern))
45
46    return result

Key Differences from Standard Subsequence:

Aspect Subsequence (LC 392) CamelCase Matching (LC 1023)
Pattern Any subsequence Subsequence with type constraint
Non-match chars Ignored MUST be lowercase
Uppercase non-match Ignored Instant failure
Use case General matching Identifier/name matching

Visualization:

1Pattern = "FB"
2
3Query 1: "FooBar"
4  F → match ✓
5  o → lowercase non-match ✓
6  o → lowercase non-match ✓
7  B → match ✓
8  a → lowercase non-match ✓
9  r → lowercase non-match ✓
10  Result: TRUE
11
12Query 2: "FooBarTest"
13  F → match ✓
14  o → lowercase non-match ✓
15  o → lowercase non-match ✓
16  B → match ✓
17  a → lowercase non-match ✓
18  r → lowercase non-match ✓
19  T → UPPERCASE non-match ✗ FAIL!
20  Result: FALSE

Pointer Movement Rules:

  1. i (Query Explorer):

    • Moves forward EVERY step
    • Scans every character in query
    • Never goes backward

  2. j (Pattern Goal Tracker):

    • ONLY moves when finding matching character
    • If j == pattern.length(), all pattern chars found

  3. Safety Check:

    • Non-matching uppercase → immediate return false
    • Non-matching lowercase → continue (allowed insertion)

Classic Problems:

  • LC 1023 Camelcase Matching (this pattern)
  • LC 392 Is Subsequence (simpler version)
  • LC 524 Longest Word in Dictionary through Deleting
  • LC 792 Number of Matching Subsequences

0-2-3) QuickSelect (Partition Algorithm for Kth Element)

Pattern Overview: QuickSelect is a selection algorithm to find the Kth smallest/largest element in an unordered list. It’s related to QuickSort but only recurses into one side of the partition. This makes it O(n) average time instead of O(n log n).

Core Concept:

1Given array: [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], find 2nd largest (k=2)
2
3QuickSort: Sorts entire array → O(n log n)
4QuickSelect: Only finds the Kth element position → O(n) average

Key Insight:

  • After partitioning around a pivot, the pivot is in its final sorted position
  • If pivot index = k, we found the answer
  • If pivot index < k, search right partition
  • If pivot index > k, search left partition

Algorithm Steps:

  1. Choose a pivot (usually last element, or random for better performance)
  2. Partition array: elements < pivot on left, elements > pivot on right
  3. If pivot position == k, return pivot value
  4. If pivot position < k, recursively search right partition
  5. If pivot position > k, recursively search left partition

Template 1: Kth Largest Element

1# Python - QuickSelect for Kth Largest
2def findKthLargest(nums, k):
3    """
4    Find Kth largest element using QuickSelect.
5
6    Time: O(n) average, O(n^2) worst (if bad pivots)
7    Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive
8
9    Key: Kth largest means (n - k)th smallest in 0-indexed array
10    """
11    def partition(left, right):
12        """
13        Partition using last element as pivot.
14        Returns pivot's final position.
15        """
16        pivot = nums[right]
17        i = left  # Position where elements < pivot should go
18
19        # Move all elements < pivot to the left
20        for j in range(left, right):
21            if nums[j] < pivot:
22                nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
23                i += 1
24
25        # Place pivot in correct position
26        nums[i], nums[right] = nums[right], nums[i]
27        return i
28
29    def quickselect(left, right, k_smallest):
30        """
31        QuickSelect to find k_smallest element (0-indexed).
32        """
33        if left == right:  # Only one element
34            return nums[left]
35
36        # Partition and get pivot position
37        pivot_idx = partition(left, right)
38
39        # Check if we found the answer
40        if pivot_idx == k_smallest:
41            return nums[pivot_idx]
42        elif pivot_idx < k_smallest:
43            # Search right partition
44            return quickselect(pivot_idx + 1, right, k_smallest)
45        else:
46            # Search left partition
47            return quickselect(left, pivot_idx - 1, k_smallest)
48
49    # Kth largest = (n - k)th smallest (0-indexed)
50    n = len(nums)
51    return quickselect(0, n - 1, n - k)
52
53# Example usage:
54# nums = [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], k = 2
55# Result: 5 (2nd largest)

1// Java - QuickSelect for Kth Largest
2/**
3 * LC 215 - Kth Largest Element in an Array
4 *
5 * time = O(N) average, O(N^2) worst
6 * space = O(1) iterative, O(log N) recursive
7 */
8class Solution {
9    public int findKthLargest(int[] nums, int k) {
10        int n = nums.length;
11        // Kth largest = (n - k)th smallest (0-indexed)
12        return quickSelect(nums, 0, n - 1, n - k);
13    }
14
15    private int quickSelect(int[] nums, int left, int right, int kSmallest) {
16        if (left == right) {
17            return nums[left];
18        }
19
20        // Partition and get pivot position
21        int pivotIdx = partition(nums, left, right);
22
23        // Check if we found the answer
24        if (pivotIdx == kSmallest) {
25            return nums[pivotIdx];
26        } else if (pivotIdx < kSmallest) {
27            // Search right partition
28            return quickSelect(nums, pivotIdx + 1, right, kSmallest);
29        } else {
30            // Search left partition
31            return quickSelect(nums, left, pivotIdx - 1, kSmallest);
32        }
33    }
34
35    private int partition(int[] nums, int left, int right) {
36        // Use last element as pivot
37        int pivot = nums[right];
38        int i = left;  // Position for elements < pivot
39
40        // Move all elements < pivot to the left
41        for (int j = left; j < right; j++) {
42            if (nums[j] < pivot) {
43                swap(nums, i, j);
44                i++;
45            }
46        }
47
48        // Place pivot in correct position
49        swap(nums, i, right);
50        return i;
51    }
52
53    private void swap(int[] nums, int i, int j) {
54        int temp = nums[i];
55        nums[i] = nums[j];
56        nums[j] = temp;
57    }
58}

Visual Example: Finding 2nd Largest in [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4]

1Target: k = 2 (2nd largest)
2Array: [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4]
3n = 6, so we need (n - k) = 4th smallest element (0-indexed)
4
5Step 1: Partition with pivot = 4 (last element)
6[3, 2, 1, 4, 6, 5]
7         ↑
8    pivot_idx = 3
9
10  Elements < 4: [3, 2, 1]
11  Pivot: 4 (at index 3)
12  Elements > 4: [6, 5]
13
14Check: pivot_idx (3) < k_smallest (4)
15Action: Search right partition [6, 5]
16
17Step 2: Partition right side [6, 5] with pivot = 5
18[3, 2, 1, 4, 5, 6]
19            ↑
20    pivot_idx = 4
21
22Check: pivot_idx (4) == k_smallest (4) ✓
23Answer: nums[4] = 5 (2nd largest element)

Template 2: K Closest Points to Origin (LC 973)

1# Python - K Closest Points using QuickSelect
2def kClosest(points, k):
3    """
4    Find K closest points to origin using QuickSelect.
5
6    Time: O(n) average
7    Space: O(1)
8    """
9    def distance(point):
10        return point[0] ** 2 + point[1] ** 2
11
12    def partition(left, right):
13        pivot_dist = distance(points[right])
14        i = left
15
16        for j in range(left, right):
17            if distance(points[j]) < pivot_dist:
18                points[i], points[j] = points[j], points[i]
19                i += 1
20
21        points[i], points[right] = points[right], points[i]
22        return i
23
24    def quickselect(left, right, k):
25        if left == right:
26            return
27
28        pivot_idx = partition(left, right)
29
30        if pivot_idx == k:
31            return
32        elif pivot_idx < k:
33            quickselect(pivot_idx + 1, right, k)
34        else:
35            quickselect(left, pivot_idx - 1, k)
36
37    # Find K smallest distances
38    quickselect(0, len(points) - 1, k - 1)
39    return points[:k]

1// Java - K Closest Points
2/**
3 * LC 973 - K Closest Points to Origin
4 *
5 * time = O(N) average
6 * space = O(1)
7 */
8class Solution {
9    public int[][] kClosest(int[][] points, int k) {
10        quickSelect(points, 0, points.length - 1, k - 1);
11        return Arrays.copyOfRange(points, 0, k);
12    }
13
14    private void quickSelect(int[][] points, int left, int right, int k) {
15        if (left >= right) return;
16
17        int pivotIdx = partition(points, left, right);
18
19        if (pivotIdx == k) {
20            return;
21        } else if (pivotIdx < k) {
22            quickSelect(points, pivotIdx + 1, right, k);
23        } else {
24            quickSelect(points, left, pivotIdx - 1, k);
25        }
26    }
27
28    private int partition(int[][] points, int left, int right) {
29        int[] pivot = points[right];
30        int pivotDist = distance(pivot);
31        int i = left;
32
33        for (int j = left; j < right; j++) {
34            if (distance(points[j]) < pivotDist) {
35                swap(points, i, j);
36                i++;
37            }
38        }
39
40        swap(points, i, right);
41        return i;
42    }
43
44    private int distance(int[] point) {
45        return point[0] * point[0] + point[1] * point[1];
46    }
47
48    private void swap(int[][] points, int i, int j) {
49        int[] temp = points[i];
50        points[i] = points[j];
51        points[j] = temp;
52    }
53}

Optimization: Randomized Pivot

1# Randomized QuickSelect for better average performance
2import random
3
4def findKthLargest_randomized(nums, k):
5    """
6    Randomized pivot selection reduces worst-case probability.
7
8    Time: O(n) average with high probability
9    """
10    def partition(left, right):
11        # RANDOM pivot selection
12        random_idx = random.randint(left, right)
13        nums[random_idx], nums[right] = nums[right], nums[random_idx]
14
15        pivot = nums[right]
16        i = left
17
18        for j in range(left, right):
19            if nums[j] < pivot:
20                nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
21                i += 1
22
23        nums[i], nums[right] = nums[right], nums[i]
24        return i
25
26    def quickselect(left, right, k_smallest):
27        if left == right:
28            return nums[left]
29
30        pivot_idx = partition(left, right)
31
32        if pivot_idx == k_smallest:
33            return nums[pivot_idx]
34        elif pivot_idx < k_smallest:
35            return quickselect(pivot_idx + 1, right, k_smallest)
36        else:
37            return quickselect(left, pivot_idx - 1, k_smallest)
38
39    n = len(nums)
40    return quickselect(0, n - 1, n - k)

Partition Algorithm Variants

1. Hoare Partition (Two-Pointer from Ends):

1def partition_hoare(nums, left, right):
2    """
3    Hoare's partition: pointers move from both ends.
4    More efficient with fewer swaps.
5    """
6    pivot = nums[(left + right) // 2]  # Middle element as pivot
7    i, j = left - 1, right + 1
8
9    while True:
10        # Move i right until element >= pivot
11        i += 1
12        while nums[i] < pivot:
13            i += 1
14
15        # Move j left until element <= pivot
16        j -= 1
17        while nums[j] > pivot:
18            j -= 1
19
20        if i >= j:
21            return j
22
23        nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]

2. Lomuto Partition (Single Pass):

1def partition_lomuto(nums, left, right):
2    """
3    Lomuto's partition: single pointer from left.
4    Simpler but may do more swaps.
5    """
6    pivot = nums[right]
7    i = left
8
9    for j in range(left, right):
10        if nums[j] <= pivot:
11            nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
12            i += 1
13
14    nums[i], nums[right] = nums[right], nums[i]
15    return i

Classic LeetCode Problems

Problem LC# Difficulty Variant Key Insight
Kth Largest Element in Array 215 Medium Basic QuickSelect Find (n-k)th smallest
K Closest Points to Origin 973 Medium Custom comparator Partition by distance
Top K Frequent Elements 347 Medium With frequency map QuickSelect on frequencies
Top K Frequent Words 692 Medium With frequency + trie QuickSelect + lexicographic order
Kth Largest Element in Stream 703 Easy Min heap alternative QuickSelect for initialization
Find Kth Smallest Pair Distance 719 Hard Binary search on answer Not direct QuickSelect
Wiggle Sort II 324 Medium 3-way partition Dutch national flag variant
Sort Colors 75 Medium 3-way partition Dutch national flag
Kth Smallest Element in BST 230 Medium In-order traversal Not QuickSelect (tree structure)
Find Median from Data Stream 295 Hard Two heaps QuickSelect alternative

Performance Comparison

Algorithm Average Time Worst Time Space Use Case
QuickSelect O(n) O(n²) O(1) Find Kth element (unsorted)
QuickSelect (Randomized) O(n) O(n²) low prob O(1) Better average performance
Heap (Min/Max) O(n log k) O(n log k) O(k) Online/streaming data
Full Sort O(n log n) O(n log n) O(1) or O(n) Need sorted array anyway
Counting Sort O(n + k) O(n + k) O(k) Small integer range

When to Use QuickSelect:

  • ✅ Need exactly Kth element, don’t need full sort
  • ✅ Can modify input array (in-place)
  • ✅ Offline algorithm (all data available)
  • ✅ Large dataset where O(n) vs O(n log n) matters

When NOT to Use QuickSelect:

  • ❌ Need all K elements in sorted order → Use heap or full sort
  • ❌ Online/streaming data → Use heap
  • ❌ Cannot modify input array → Use heap
  • ❌ Worst-case guarantee needed → Use Median of Medians (O(n) worst-case)

Interview Tips

1. Common Mistakes:

  • Forgetting to convert “Kth largest” → “(n - k)th smallest”
  • Off-by-one errors with 0-indexed vs 1-indexed k
  • Not handling left == right base case
  • Infinite recursion when partition doesn’t move pivot

2. Optimization Techniques:

  • Randomized pivot: Reduces worst-case probability
  • Median-of-three: Choose median of first, middle, last elements as pivot
  • Iterative version: Avoid stack overflow for very large arrays
  • Tail recursion: Only recurse into smaller partition

3. Complexity Analysis:

1Best/Average Case: O(n + n/2 + n/4 + ... + 1) = O(2n) = O(n)
2
3Worst Case (bad pivots every time):
4  O(n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1) = O(n²)
5
6With randomized pivot:
7  Worst case O(n²) probability → near zero for large n

4. Interview Talking Points:

  • “QuickSelect is like QuickSort but only recurses into one partition”
  • “Average O(n) is better than O(n log k) heap for finding single Kth element”
  • “Trade-off: Modifies array vs. heap keeps original”
  • “Randomized pivot gives O(n) with high probability”

5. Follow-up Questions:

  • Q: “What if we need all K elements sorted?”
    • A: Use heap (O(n log k)) or partial QuickSort
  • Q: “What if array is read-only?”
    • A: Copy to new array or use heap
  • Q: “Can we guarantee O(n) worst-case?”
    • A: Yes, using Median of Medians algorithm (complex, rarely asked)

Advanced: Median of Medians (O(n) Worst-Case)

1def findKthLargest_median_of_medians(nums, k):
2    """
3    Guaranteed O(n) worst-case using Median of Medians pivot selection.
4    More complex but theoretically optimal.
5
6    Time: O(n) worst-case
7    Space: O(log n) recursion
8    """
9    def median_of_medians(left, right):
10        """Find approximate median for good pivot."""
11        if right - left < 5:
12            return sorted(nums[left:right + 1])[len(nums[left:right + 1]) // 2]
13
14        # Divide into groups of 5, find median of each
15        medians = []
16        for i in range(left, right + 1, 5):
17            sub_right = min(i + 4, right)
18            median = sorted(nums[i:sub_right + 1])[(sub_right - i) // 2]
19            medians.append(median)
20
21        # Recursively find median of medians
22        return median_of_medians_list(medians)
23
24    def partition(left, right, pivot_value):
25        # Partition around pivot_value
26        # ... implementation ...
27        pass
28
29    # Main quickselect with median of medians pivot
30    # ... implementation ...
31    pass

Note: Median of Medians is rarely implemented in interviews due to complexity. Randomized QuickSelect is preferred in practice.

1) General form

1-1) Basic OP

1-1-1 : Reverse Array

1// java
2void reverse(int[] nums){
3
4    int left = 0;
5    int right = nums.length - 1
6
7    while (left < right){
8
9        int tmp = nums(left);
10        nums(left) = nums(right)
11        nums(right) = tmp;
12        
13        left += 1;
14        right -= 1;
15    }
16}

1-1-6: binary search

1-1-7: sliding window

2) LC Example

2-1) Remove Element

1# python
2# basic
3class Solution(object):
4    def removeElement(self, nums, val):
5        length = 0
6        for i in range(len(nums)):
7            if nums[i] != val:
8                nums[length] = nums[i]
9                length += 1
10        return length

1# LC 026 : Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
2# https://github.com/yennanliu/CS_basics/blob/master/leetcode_python/Array/remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.py
3# V0
4# IDEA : 2 POINTERS: i, j
5class Solution(object):
6    def removeDuplicates(self, nums):
7        # edge case
8        if not nums:
9            return
10        i = 0
11        for j in range(1, len(nums)):
12            """
13            NOTE !!!
14             -> note this condition
15             -> we HAVE to swap i+1, j once nums[i], nums[j] are different
16             -> so we MAKE SURE there is no duplicate
17            """
18            if nums[j] != nums[i]:
19                nums[i+1], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i+1]
20                i += 1
21
22        #print ("nums = " + str(nums))
23        return i+1
24
25# V0'
26# IDEA : 2 POINTERS
27# HAVE A POINTER j STARTS FROM 0 AND THE OTHER POINTER i GO THROUGH nums
28#  -> IF A[i] != A[j]
29#     -> THEN SWITCH A[i] AND A[j+1]
30#     -> and j += 1
31# *** NOTE : it's swith A[j+1] (j+1) with A[i]
32# DEMO 1 
33# A = [1,1,1,2,3]
34# s = Solution()
35# s.removeDuplicates(A)
36# [1, 1, 1, 2, 3]
37# [1, 1, 1, 2, 3]
38# [1, 1, 1, 2, 3]
39# [1, 2, 1, 1, 3]
40# [1, 2, 3, 1, 1]
41#
42# DEMO 2
43# A = [1,2,2,3,4]
44# s = Solution()
45# s.removeDuplicates(A)
46# A = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4]
47# A = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4]
48# A = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4]
49# A = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4]
50# A = [1, 2, 3, 2, 4]
51# -> A = [1, 2, 3, 4, 2]
52class Solution:
53    def removeDuplicates(self, A):
54        if len(A) == 0:
55            return 0
56        j = 0
57        for i in range(0, len(A)):
58            ###  NOTE : below condition
59            if A[i] != A[j]:
60                A[i], A[j+1] = A[j+1], A[i]
61                j = j + 1
62        return j+1

1# LC 283 move-zeroes
2# V0
3class Solution(object):
4    def moveZeroes(self, nums):
5        y = 0
6        for x in range(len(nums)):
7            if nums[x] != 0:
8                nums[x], nums[y] = nums[y], nums[x]
9                y += 1
10        return nums
11
12# V0'
13class Solution(object):
14    def moveZeroes(self, nums):
15        # edge case
16        if not nums:
17            return
18        j = 0
19        for i in range(1, len(nums)):
20            # if nums[j] = 0, swap with nums[i]
21            if nums[j] == 0:
22                if nums[i] != 0:
23                    nums[j], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[j]
24                    j += 1
25            # if nums[j] != 0, then move j (j+=1) for searching next 0
26            else:
27                j += 1
28        return nums

1# LC 080 : Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II
2# V0
3# IDEA : 2 POINTERS
4#### NOTE : THE nums already ordering
5# DEMO
6# example 1
7# nums = [1,1,1,2,2,3]
8#           i j
9#           i   j
10#        [1,1,2,1,2,3]
11#             i   j
12#        [1,1,2,2,1,3]
13#               i   j
14#
15# example 2
16# nums = [0,0,1,1,1,1,2,3,3] 
17#           i j
18#        [0,0,1,1,1,1,2,3,3]
19#             i j
20#        [0,0,1,1,1,1,2,3,3]
21#               i j
22#        [0,0,1,1,1,1,2,3,3]
23#               i   j
24#               i     j
25#        [0,0,1,1,2,1,1,3,3]
26#                 i     j  
27#        [0,0,1,1,2,3,1,1,3]
28#                   i     j
29#        [0,0,1,1,2,3,3,1,1]
30class Solution:
31    def removeDuplicates(self, nums):
32        if len(nums) < 3:
33            return len(nums)
34
35        ### NOTE : slow starts from 1
36        slow = 1
37        ### NOTE : fast starts from 2
38        for fast in range(2, len(nums)):
39            """
40            NOTE : BELOW CONDITION
41
42            1) nums[slow] != nums[fast]: for adding "1st" element
43            2) nums[slow] != nums[slow-1] : for adding "2nd" element
44            """
45            if nums[slow] != nums[fast] or nums[slow] != nums[slow-1]:
46                # both of below op are OK
47                #nums[slow+1] = nums[fast]
48                nums[slow+1], nums[fast] = nums[fast], nums[slow+1] 
49                slow += 1
50        return slow+1

2-2) Longest Palindromic Substring

1# LC 005 Longest Palindromic Substring
2# V0
3# IDEA : TWO POINTERS
4# -> DEAL WITH odd, even len cases
5#  -> step 1) for loop on idx 
6#  -> step 2) and start from "center" 
7#  -> step 3) and do a while loop
8#  -> step 4) check if len of sub str > 1
9# https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-substring/discuss/1025355/Easy-to-understand-solution-with-O(n2)-time-complexity
10# Time complexity = best case O(n) to worse case O(n^2)
11# Space complexity = O(1) if not considering the space complexity for result, as all the comparison happens in place.
12class Solution:
13    # The logic I have used is very simple, iterate over each character in the array and assming that its the center of a palindrome step in either direction to see how far you can go by keeping the property of palindrome true. The trick is that the palindrome can be of odd or even length and in each case the center will be different.
14    # For odd length palindrome i am considering the index being iterating on is the center, thereby also catching the scenario of a palindrome with a length of 1.
15    # For even length palindrome I am considering the index being iterating over and the next element on the left is the center.
16    def longestPalindrome(self, s):
17
18        if len(s) <= 1:
19            return s
20
21        res = []
22
23        for idx in range(len(s)):
24        
25            """
26            # CASE 1) : odd len
27            # Check for odd length palindrome with idx at its center
28
29            -> NOTE : the only difference (between odd, even len)
30            
31            -> NOTE !!!  : 2 idx : left = right = idx
32            """
33            left = right = idx
34            # note the condition !!!
35            while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]:
36                if right - left + 1 > len(res):
37                    res = s[left:right + 1]
38                left -= 1
39                right += 1
40              
41            """"
42            # CASE 2) : even len  
43            # Check for even length palindrome with idx and idx-1 as its center
44
45            -> NOTE : the only difference (between odd, even len)
46
47            -> NOTE !!!  : 2 idx : left = idx - 1,  right = idx
48            """
49            left = idx - 1
50            right = idx
51            # note the condition !!!
52            while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]:
53                if right - left + 1 > len(res):
54                    res = s[left:right + 1]
55                left -= 1
56                right += 1
57
58        return res
59
60# V0'
61# IDEA : TWO POINTER + RECURSION
62# https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-substring/discuss/1057629/Python.-Super-simple-and-easy-understanding-solution.-O(n2).
63class Solution:
64    def longestPalindrome(self, s):
65        res = ""
66        length = len(s)
67        def helper(left, right):
68            while left >= 0 and right < length and s[left] == s[right]:
69                left -= 1
70                right += 1      
71            return s[left + 1 : right]
72        
73        for index in range(len(s)):
74            res = max(helper(index, index), helper(index, index + 1), res, key = len)           
75        return res

2-3) Container With Most Water

1# LC 11 Container With Most Water
2# V0 
3# IDEA : TWO POINTERS 
4class Solution(object):
5    def maxArea(self, height):
6        ans = 0
7        l = 0
8        r = len(height) - 1
9        while l < r:
10            ans = max(ans, min(height[l], height[r]) * (r - l))
11            if height[l] < height[r]:
12                l += 1
13            else:
14                r -= 1
15        return ans

2-4) Longest Consecutive Sequence

1# LC 128 Longest Consecutive Sequence
2
3# V0
4# IDEA : sliding window
5class Solution(object):
6    def longestConsecutive(self, nums):
7        # edge case
8        if not nums:
9            return 0
10        nums = list(set(nums))
11        # if len(nums) == 1: # not necessary
12        #     return 1
13        # sort first
14        nums.sort()
15        res = 0
16        l = 0
17        r = 1
18        """
19        NOTE !!!
20
21        Sliding window here :
22            condition :  l, r are still in list (r < len(nums) and l < len(nums))
23
24            2 cases
25
26                case 1) nums[r] != nums[r-1] + 1
27                    -> means not continous, 
28                        -> so we need to move r to right (1 idx)
29                        -> and MOVE l to r - 1, since it's NOT possible to have any continous subarray within [l, r] anymore
30                case 2) nums[r] == nums[r-1] + 1
31                        -> means there is continous subarray currently, so we keep moving r to right (r+=1) and get current max sub array length (res = max(res, r-l+1))
32        """
33        while r < len(nums) and l < len(nums):
34            # case 1)
35            if nums[r] != nums[r-1] + 1:
36                r += 1
37                l = (r-1)
38            # case 2)
39            else:
40                res = max(res, r-l+1)
41                r += 1
42        # edge case : if res == 0, means no continous array (with len > 1), so we return 1 (a single alphabet can be recognized as a "continous assay", and its len = 1)
43        return res if res > 1 else 1
44
45# V0'
46# IDEA : SORTING + 2 POINTERS
47class Solution(object):
48    def longestConsecutive(self, nums):
49        # edge case
50        if not nums:
51            return 0
52
53        nums.sort()
54        cur_len = 1
55        max_len = 1
56        #print ("nums = " + str(nums))
57
58        # NOTE : start from idx = 1
59        for i in range(1, len(nums)):
60            ### NOTE : start from nums[i] != nums[i-1] case
61            if nums[i] != nums[i-1]:
62                ### NOTE : if nums[i] == nums[i-1]+1 : cur_len += 1
63                if nums[i] == nums[i-1]+1:
64                    cur_len += 1
65                ### NOTE : if nums[i] != nums[i-1]+1 : get max len, and reset cur_lent as 1
66                else:
67                    max_len = max(max_len, cur_len)
68                    cur_len = 1
69        # check max len again
70        return max(max_len, cur_len)

2-6) Palindromic Substrings

1# LC 647. Palindromic Substrings
2# V0'
3# IDEA : TWO POINTERS
4# https://leetcode.com/problems/palindromic-substrings/discuss/1041760/Python-Easy-Solution-Beats-85
5# https://github.com/yennanliu/CS_basics/blob/master/leetcode_python/String/longest-palindromic-substring.py
6class Solution:
7    def countSubstrings(self, s):
8        ans = 0    
9        for i in range(len(s)):
10            # odd
11            ans += self.helper(s, i, i)
12            # even
13            ans += self.helper(s, i, i + 1)  
14        return ans
15        
16    def helper(self, s, l, r):     
17        ans = 0    
18        while l >= 0 and r < len(s) and s[l] == s[r]:
19            l -= 1
20            r += 1
21            ans += 1          
22        return ans
23
24# V0
25# IDEA : BRUTE FORCE
26class Solution(object):
27    def countSubstrings(self, s):
28        count = 0
29        # NOTE: since i from 0 to len(s) - 1, so for j we need to "+1" then can get go throgh all elements in str
30        for i in range(len(s)):
31            # Note : for j we need to "+1"
32            for j in range(i+1, len(s)+1):
33                if s[i:j] == s[i:j][::-1]:
34                    count += 1
35        return count
36
37# V0''
38# IDEA : TWO POINTERS (similar as LC 005)
39class Solution(object):
40    def countSubstrings(self, s):
41        count = 0
42        for i in range(len(s)):
43            # for every single character
44            count += 1
45            
46            # case 1) palindromic substrings length is odd 
47            left = i - 1
48            right = i + 1
49            while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]:
50                count += 1
51                left -= 1
52                right += 1
53
54            # case 2) palindromic substrings length is even 
55            left = i - 1
56            right = i
57            while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]:
58                count += 1
59                left -= 1
60                right += 1
61
62        return count

2-7) Sum of Subarray Ranges

1# LC 2104. Sum of Subarray Ranges
2# V0
3# IDEA : BRUTE FORCE
4class Solution:
5    def subArrayRanges(self, nums):
6        res = 0
7        for i in range(len(nums)):
8            curMin = float("inf")
9            curMax = -float("inf")
10            for j in range(i, len(nums)):
11                curMin = min(curMin, nums[j])
12                curMax = max(curMax, nums[j])
13                res += curMax - curMin
14        return res
15
16# V0'
17# IDEA : INCREASING STACK
18class Solution:
19    def subArrayRanges(self, A0):
20        res = 0
21        inf = float('inf')
22        A = [-inf] + A0 + [-inf]
23        s = []
24        for i, x in enumerate(A):
25            while s and A[s[-1]] > x:
26                j = s.pop()
27                k = s[-1]
28                res -= A[j] * (i - j) * (j - k)
29            s.append(i)
30            
31        A = [inf] + A0 + [inf]
32        s = []
33        for i, x in enumerate(A):
34            while s and A[s[-1]] < x:
35                j = s.pop()
36                k = s[-1]
37                res += A[j] * (i - j) * (j - k)
38            s.append(i)
39        return res

2-8) Trapping Rain Water

1# LC 42. Trapping Rain Water
2# NOTE : there is also 2 scan, dp approaches
3# V0'
4# IDEA : TWO POINTERS 
5# IDEA : CORE
6#     -> step 1) use left_max, right_mex : record "highest" "wall" in left, right handside at current idx
7#     -> step 2) 
8#                case 2-1) if height[left] < height[right] : 
9#                   -> all left passed idx's height is LOWER than height[right]
10#                   -> so the "short" wall MUST on left
11#                   -> and since we record left_max, so we can get trap amount based on left_max, height[left]
12#                
13#                case 2-2) if height[left] > height[right]
14#                   -> .... (similar as above)
15class Solution:
16    def trap(self, height):
17 
18        if not height:
19            return 0
20
21        left_max = right_max = res = 0
22        left, right = 0, len(height) - 1
23 
24        while left < right:
25            if height[left] < height[right]:  # left pointer op
26                if height[left] < left_max:
27                    res += left_max - height[left]
28                else:
29                    left_max = height[left]
30                left += 1  # move left pointer 
31            else:
32                if height[right] < right_max:  # right pointer op
33                    res += right_max - height[right]
34                else:
35                    right_max = height[right]
36                right -= 1  # move right pointer 
37        return res

2-9) Next Permutation

1# LC 31. Next Permutation
2# V0
3class Solution:
4    def nextPermutation(self, nums):
5        i = j = len(nums)-1
6        while i > 0 and nums[i-1] >= nums[i]:
7            i -= 1
8        if i == 0:   # nums are in descending order
9            nums.reverse()
10            return 
11        k = i - 1    # find the last "ascending" position
12        while nums[j] <= nums[k]:
13            j -= 1
14        nums[k], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[k]  
15        l, r = k+1, len(nums)-1  # reverse the second part
16        while l < r:
17            nums[l], nums[r] = nums[r], nums[l]
18            l +=1
19            r -= 1
20
21# V0'
22class Solution(object):
23    def nextPermutation(self, num):
24        k, l = -1, 0
25        for i in range(len(num) - 1):
26            if num[i] < num[i + 1]:
27                k = i
28
29        if k == -1:
30            num.reverse()
31            return
32
33        for i in range(k + 1, len(num)):
34            if num[i] > num[k]:
35                l = i
36        num[k], num[l] = num[l], num[k]
37        num[k + 1:] = num[:k:-1] ### dounle check here ###

2-10) Valid Palindrome II (Palindrome with One Deletion)

Pattern: Two Pointers with Mismatch Handling

  • Check palindrome from both ends
  • On first mismatch, try TWO possibilities:
    1. Skip left character (check s[l+1...r])
    2. Skip right character (check s[l...r-1])
  • If EITHER works, return true
  • Use helper function to check palindrome in range

Key Insight:

  • Don’t remove character and create new string (O(N) space)
  • Instead, use pointers to check substring in-place (O(1) space)
1// java
2// LC 680. Valid Palindrome II
3/**
4 * Pattern: Palindrome with at most 1 deletion allowed
5 *
6 * Example:
7 *   s = "abca"
8 *
9 *   [a b c a]    l=0, r=3, s[l]=a, s[r]=a, match! l++, r--
10 *    l     r
11 *
12 *   [a b c a]    l=1, r=2, s[l]=b, s[r]=c, MISMATCH!
13 *      l r       Try: skip b (check "ca") OR skip c (check "ba")
14 *                     "ca" is NOT palindrome
15 *                     "ba" is NOT palindrome
16 *                BUT we need to check full substring!
17 *
18 *   Actually for "abca":
19 *   - Try skip l: check "cba" -> isPali("abca", 2, 3) = true (just "a")
20 *   - OR skip r: check "aba" -> isPali("abca", 1, 2) = true (just "b")
21 *
22 *   Either works -> return true
23 *
24 * Time: O(N), Space: O(1)
25 */
26public boolean validPalindrome(String s) {
27    int l = 0;
28    int r = s.length() - 1;
29
30    while (l < r) {
31        if (s.charAt(l) != s.charAt(r)) {
32            /** NOTE !!!
33             *
34             *  On mismatch, try BOTH possibilities:
35             *    1. Skip left char  -> check s[l+1...r]
36             *    2. Skip right char -> check s[l...r-1]
37             *
38             *  If EITHER is palindrome, we can make it work with 1 deletion
39             */
40            return isPalindrome(s, l + 1, r) || isPalindrome(s, l, r - 1);
41        }
42        l++;
43        r--;
44    }
45
46    return true; // Already a perfect palindrome
47}
48
49/** NOTE !!!
50 *
51 *  Helper function with left, right pointers as parameters
52 *  Checks if substring s[l...r] is palindrome
53 *  NO new string created - check in place!
54 */
55private boolean isPalindrome(String s, int l, int r) {
56    while (l < r) {
57        if (s.charAt(l) != s.charAt(r)) {
58            return false;
59        }
60        l++;
61        r--;
62    }
63    return true;
64}

1# python
2# LC 680. Valid Palindrome II
3class Solution:
4    def validPalindrome(self, s):
5
6        l, r = 0, len(s) - 1
7
8        while l < r:
9            if s[l] != s[r]:
10                """
11                # NOTE this !!!!
12                -> On mismatch, try skipping left OR right character
13                -> Check if either resulting substring is palindrome
14                """
15                skip_left = s[l+1:r+1]   # skip s[l]
16                skip_right = s[l:r]      # skip s[r]
17                # NOTE this !!!!
18                return skip_left == skip_left[::-1] or skip_right == skip_right[::-1]
19            else:
20                l += 1
21                r -= 1
22
23        return True

Common Mistakes:

  • ❌ Creating new strings (O(N) space and time)
  • ❌ Only trying to skip one side
  • ✅ Use helper with pointers (O(1) space)
  • ✅ Try BOTH skip possibilities

Similar Problems:

  • LC 680 Valid Palindrome II (this pattern)
  • LC 125 Valid Palindrome
  • LC 1216 Valid Palindrome III (k deletions allowed - DP)
  • LC 234 Palindrome Linked List

2-11) Merge Sorted Array

1# LC 88. Merge Sorted Array
2# V0
3# IDEA : 2 pointers
4### NOTE : we need to merge the sorted arrat to nums1 with IN PLACE (CAN'T USE EXTRA CACHE)
5# -> SO WE START FROM RIGHT HAND SIDE (biggeest element) to LEFT HAND SIDE (smallest element)
6# -> Then paste the remain elements
7class Solution(object):
8    def merge(self, nums1, m, nums2, n):
9        ### NOTE : we define 2 pointers (p, q) here
10        p, q = m-1, n-1
11        ### NOTE : the while loop conditions
12        while p >= 0 and q >= 0:
13            if nums1[p] > nums2[q]:
14                #***** NOTE : WE START FROM p+q+1 index, since that's the count of non-zero elements in nums1, and nums2
15                nums1[p+q+1] = nums1[p]
16                p = p-1
17            else:
18                ### NOTE WE START FROM p+q+1 index, reason same as above
19                nums1[p+q+1] = nums2[q]
20                q = q-1
21        # if there're still elements in nums2, we just replace the ones in nums1[:q+1] with them (nums2[:q+1])
22        nums1[:q+1] = nums2[:q+1]

2-12) Interval List Intersections

1// java
2// LC 986
3    public int[][] intervalIntersection_1(int[][] firstList, int[][] secondList) {
4        if (firstList.length == 0 || secondList.length == 0)
5            return new int[0][0];
6    /**
7     *  NOTE !!!!
8     *   - i and j are pointers used to iterate through
9     *      `firstList` and `secondList` respectively.
10     *
11     *   - `startMax` and `endMin` are used to compute
12     *     the `intersection` of the current intervals
13     *     from firstList and secondList.
14     *
15     *   - ans is a list to store the resulting intersection intervals.
16     */
17        int i = 0;
18        int j = 0;
19        int startMax = 0, endMin = 0;
20        List<int[]> ans = new ArrayList<>();
21
22    /**
23     *
24     *   - The loop continues as long as
25     *      there are intervals remaining in `BOTH lists`.
26     *
27     *   - `startMax` is the maximum of the `START points` of the two
28     *     intervals (firstList[i] and secondList[j]).
29     *       -> This ensures the intersection starts no earlier than both intervals.
30     *
31     *   - `endMin` is the minimum of the `END points` of the two intervals.
32     *
33     *   - This ensures the intersection ends no later than the earlier of
34     *     the two intervals.
35     *
36     */
37    while (i < firstList.length && j < secondList.length) {
38      startMax = Math.max(firstList[i][0], secondList[j][0]);
39      endMin = Math.min(firstList[i][1], secondList[j][1]);
40
41      // you have end greater than start and you already know that this interval is
42      // surrounded with startMin and endMax so this must be the intersection
43      /**
44       *
45       *  - If endMin >= startMax, it means there is an intersection between the two intervals.
46       *    ->  Add the intersection [startMax, endMin] to the result list.
47       */
48      if (endMin >= startMax) {
49        ans.add(new int[] {startMax, endMin});
50      }
51
52      // the interval with min end has been covered completely and have no chance to
53      // intersect with any other interval so move that list's pointer
54      /**
55       * - Since the intervals are sorted and disjoint:
56       *    - If the interval from firstList ends first (or at the same time), increment i.
57       *    - If the interval from secondList ends first (or at the same time), increment j.
58       *    -> This ensures that the interval which has been fully processed is skipped, moving to the next potential candidate for intersection.
59       *
60       */
61      if (endMin == firstList[i][1]) i++;
62      if (endMin == secondList[j][1]) j++;
63        }
64
65        return ans.toArray(new int[ans.size()][2]);
66    }

2-13) Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)

Pattern: Three-Way Partitioning with Two Pointers

  • Use three pointers: left (0s), mid (current), right (2s)
  • Partition array into three sections
  • Single pass solution
1// java
2// LC 75. Sort Colors
3/**
4 * Pattern: Dutch National Flag - Three-way partitioning
5 *
6 * Goal: Sort array with only 0, 1, 2 in one pass
7 *
8 * Pointers:
9 *   - left: boundary for 0s (everything before left is 0)
10 *   - mid: current element being examined
11 *   - right: boundary for 2s (everything after right is 2)
12 *
13 * Example:
14 *   nums = [2,0,2,1,1,0]
15 *
16 *   [2,0,2,1,1,0]    mid=0, nums[mid]=2, swap with right, right--
17 *    l           r   [0,0,2,1,1,2]
18 *    m
19 *
20 *   [0,0,2,1,1,2]    mid=0, nums[mid]=0, swap with left, left++, mid++
21 *    l         r
22 *    m
23 *
24 *   [0,0,2,1,1,2]    mid=1, nums[mid]=0, swap with left, left++, mid++
25 *      l       r
26 *      m
27 *
28 *   [0,0,2,1,1,2]    mid=2, nums[mid]=2, swap with right, right--
29 *        l     r
30 *        m
31 *
32 *   [0,0,1,1,2,2]    mid=2, nums[mid]=1, mid++
33 *        l   r
34 *        m
35 *
36 *   [0,0,1,1,2,2]    mid=3, nums[mid]=1, mid++
37 *        l r
38 *          m
39 *
40 *   mid > right, done!
41 *
42 * Time: O(N), Space: O(1)
43 */
44public void sortColors(int[] nums) {
45    int left = 0;           // Next position for 0
46    int mid = 0;            // Current examining position
47    int right = nums.length - 1;  // Next position for 2
48
49    while (mid <= right) {
50        if (nums[mid] == 0) {
51            // Found 0, swap to left
52            swap(nums, left, mid);
53            left++;
54            mid++;
55        } else if (nums[mid] == 2) {
56            // Found 2, swap to right
57            // NOTE: Don't increment mid yet, need to check swapped element
58            swap(nums, mid, right);
59            right--;
60        } else {
61            // Found 1, just move mid
62            mid++;
63        }
64    }
65}
66
67private void swap(int[] nums, int i, int j) {
68    int temp = nums[i];
69    nums[i] = nums[j];
70    nums[j] = temp;
71}

Similar Problems:

  • LC 75 Sort Colors (this pattern)
  • LC 26 Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
  • LC 80 Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II
  • LC 283 Move Zeroes

2-14) 3Sum

Pattern: Two Pointers with Fixed First Element

  • Fix first element, use two pointers for remaining two
  • Avoid duplicates by skipping same values
  • Sort array first
1// java
2// LC 15. 3Sum
3/**
4 * Pattern: Fixed element + Two pointers
5 *
6 * Steps:
7 *   1. Sort array
8 *   2. Fix first element (i)
9 *   3. Use two pointers (l, r) to find remaining two elements
10 *   4. Skip duplicates
11 *
12 * Example:
13 *   nums = [-1,0,1,2,-1,-4]
14 *   After sort: [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]
15 *
16 *   i=0, nums[i]=-4, l=1, r=5
17 *   [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]
18 *     i  l       r    sum=-4+-1+2=-3 < 0, l++
19 *
20 *   i=1, nums[i]=-1, l=2, r=5
21 *   [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]
22 *        i  l     r   sum=-1+-1+2=0, found! [-1,-1,2]
23 *                     l++, r--, skip duplicates
24 *
25 *   [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]
26 *        i    l r     sum=-1+0+1=0, found! [-1,0,1]
27 *
28 * Time: O(N^2), Space: O(1) excluding result
29 */
30public List<List<Integer>> threeSum(int[] nums) {
31    List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
32    Arrays.sort(nums);
33
34    for (int i = 0; i < nums.length - 2; i++) {
35        // Skip duplicates for first element
36        if (i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i - 1]) {
37            continue;
38        }
39
40        int left = i + 1;
41        int right = nums.length - 1;
42        int target = -nums[i];
43
44        while (left < right) {
45            int sum = nums[left] + nums[right];
46
47            if (sum == target) {
48                result.add(Arrays.asList(nums[i], nums[left], nums[right]));
49
50                // Skip duplicates for second element
51                while (left < right && nums[left] == nums[left + 1]) {
52                    left++;
53                }
54                // Skip duplicates for third element
55                while (left < right && nums[right] == nums[right - 1]) {
56                    right--;
57                }
58
59                left++;
60                right--;
61            } else if (sum < target) {
62                left++;
63            } else {
64                right--;
65            }
66        }
67    }
68
69    return result;
70}

Similar Problems:

  • LC 15 3Sum (this pattern)
  • LC 16 3Sum Closest
  • LC 18 4Sum
  • LC 259 3Sum Smaller
  • LC 1 Two Sum

2-15) Reverse String / Reverse Words

Pattern: In-place Reversal with Two Pointers

1// java
2// LC 344. Reverse String
3/**
4 * Pattern: Swap from both ends moving toward center
5 *
6 * Example:
7 *   s = ['h','e','l','l','o']
8 *
9 *   ['h','e','l','l','o']
10 *     l           r       swap, l++, r--
11 *
12 *   ['o','e','l','l','h']
13 *       l       r         swap, l++, r--
14 *
15 *   ['o','l','l','e','h']
16 *           l r           l >= r, done!
17 *
18 * Time: O(N), Space: O(1)
19 */
20public void reverseString(char[] s) {
21    int left = 0;
22    int right = s.length - 1;
23
24    while (left < right) {
25        char temp = s[left];
26        s[left] = s[right];
27        s[right] = temp;
28        left++;
29        right--;
30    }
31}

Similar Problems:

  • LC 344 Reverse String
  • LC 345 Reverse Vowels of a String
  • LC 541 Reverse String II
  • LC 186 Reverse Words in a String II
  • LC 151 Reverse Words in a String

2-16) Shortest Palindrome (Find Longest Palindromic Prefix)

Pattern: Scan from right, track left pointer to find longest palindromic prefix

Core Idea:

  • Find the longest prefix of s that is already a palindrome
  • Reverse the remaining suffix and prepend it to s
  • Use two pointers: j anchored at 0 (left), i scans right-to-left
  • When s[i] == s[j], advance j — after the scan s[0..j-1] is the matched prefix
  • If j < n, recurse on s[0..j] and sandwich the non-palindrome suffix around it

Dry Run — s = "aacecaaa":

1i scans right-to-left, j starts at 0
2
3i=7 s[7]='a' == s[0]='a'  -> j=1
4i=6 s[6]='a' == s[1]='a'  -> j=2
5i=5 s[5]='a' != s[2]='c'  -> skip
6i=4 s[4]='c' == s[2]='c'  -> j=3
7i=3 s[3]='e' != s[3]='e'  -> j=4  (wait — equal!) -> j=4
8i=2 s[2]='c' != s[4]='c'  -> j=5  -> j=5
9i=1 s[1]='a' != s[5]='a'  -> j=6
10i=0 s[0]='a' != s[6]='a'  -> j=7
11
12j == n? No (j=7 < 8). suffix = s.substring(7) = "a"
13reversed("a") + shortestPalindrome("aacecaa") + "a"

Key Insight — why does j track the prefix?

1Scanning i from right to left acts like a "sieve":
2- Every time s[i] matches s[j], j advances one step right
3- After the full scan, s[0..j-1] is the longest possible palindromic prefix
4  (not a strict palindrome proof, but works with the recursive structure)
5- The characters NOT in the prefix (s[j..n-1]) form the suffix that
6  must be reversed and prepended to make the whole string a palindrome

1// java
2// LC 214. Shortest Palindrome
3/**
4 * Pattern: Find longest palindromic prefix via right-to-left scan
5 *
6 * Step 1: Scan i from n-1 to 0, advance j when s[i] == s[j]
7 * Step 2: j is now the length of the "matched" prefix
8 * Step 3: suffix  = s.substring(j)        (non-palindrome tail)
9 *         prefix  = reverse(suffix)        (chars to prepend)
10 * Step 4: return prefix + shortestPalindrome(s[0..j]) + suffix
11 *
12 * Time: O(N^2) average (O(N) per recursion level, O(N) depth)
13 * Space: O(N) recursion stack
14 *
15 * Example 1: s = "aacecaaa" -> "aaacecaaa"
16 * Example 2: s = "abcd"     -> "dcbabcd"
17 */
18public String shortestPalindrome(String s) {
19    if (s == null || s.length() <= 1) return s;
20
21    int j = 0;
22
23    /** NOTE !!!
24     *  Scan from the RIGHT end toward left.
25     *  j tracks how far into s we've "matched" from the front.
26     */
27    for (int i = s.length() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
28        if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) {
29            j++;
30        }
31    }
32
33    // Whole string is already a palindrome
34    if (j == s.length()) return s;
35
36    // suffix is the part NOT covered by the palindromic prefix
37    String suffix = s.substring(j);
38    String prefix = new StringBuilder(suffix).reverse().toString();
39
40    /** NOTE !!!
41     *  Recurse on s[0..j] to handle the inner part,
42     *  then sandwich the current suffix around it.
43     */
44    return prefix + shortestPalindrome(s.substring(0, j)) + suffix;
45}

1// java
2// LC 214. Shortest Palindrome — KMP approach (O(N) time)
3/**
4 * IDEA: KMP Prefix Table
5 *
6 * Combine s + "#" + reverse(s) into one string.
7 * The KMP prefix table's last value gives the length of the
8 * longest palindromic prefix of s.
9 *
10 * Time: O(N), Space: O(N)
11 */
12public String shortestPalindromeKMP(String s) {
13    String rev = new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString();
14    String combined = s + "#" + rev;
15    int[] table = buildPrefixTable(combined);
16
17    int palindromeLen = table[combined.length() - 1];
18    String suffix = new StringBuilder(s.substring(palindromeLen)).reverse().toString();
19    return suffix + s;
20}
21
22private int[] buildPrefixTable(String s) {
23    int[] table = new int[s.length()];
24    int len = 0;
25    for (int i = 1; i < s.length(); i++) {
26        while (len > 0 && s.charAt(i) != s.charAt(len))
27            len = table[len - 1];
28        if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(len))
29            len++;
30        table[i] = len;
31    }
32    return table;
33}

Brute-force version (TLE on large inputs):

1// java — O(N^2) brute force
2// Find largest i such that s[0..i] is a palindrome, then prepend reverse(s[i+1..n-1])
3public String shortestPalindromeBrute(String s) {
4    int n = s.length();
5    if (n <= 1) return s;
6    int end = 0;
7    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
8        if (isPalindrome(s, 0, i)) { end = i; break; }
9    }
10    String suffix = s.substring(end + 1);
11    return new StringBuilder(suffix).reverse() + s;
12}
13
14private boolean isPalindrome(String s, int l, int r) {
15    while (l < r) {
16        if (s.charAt(l++) != s.charAt(r--)) return false;
17    }
18    return true;
19}

Pointer Movement Comparison:

Approach Left pointer j Right pointer i When j advances
Right-to-left scan Anchored at 0, moves right Scans n-1 → 0 s[i] == s[j]
Brute force isPalindrome Expands from both ends Starts at n-1, decrements Always (matching chars)
KMP N/A — uses prefix table N/A N/A

4) Two Pointers Cheat Sheet

Pattern When to Use Example Problems
Opposite Direction Sorted array, palindrome check LC 167, LC 344, LC 125
Same Direction (Fast-Slow) Remove duplicates, cycle detection LC 26, LC 27, LC 142
Sliding Window Subarray/substring problems LC 3, LC 76, LC 209
Merge Two Lists Merge sorted arrays/lists LC 88, LC 21
Partition Rearrange elements LC 75, LC 86
Palindrome with Deletion Allow k changes LC 680, LC 1216
Fixed + Two Pointers Sum problems (3Sum, 4Sum) LC 15, LC 16, LC 18
Subsequence Matching Check if one string is subsequence of another LC 392, LC 524, LC 792
Pattern Match with Constraints Subsequence + character type validation LC 1023
Longest Palindromic Prefix Find longest palindromic prefix, prepend reversed suffix LC 214, LC 336
